cards.text: Unscoped enums are defined with {{c1::enum}} keyword: enum Color {{c2::{ red, green };}}}

cards.front: What integer value does the first enumerator get by default?

cards.question: What are the values of these enumerators?
cards.code: enum Color { red = 5, green, blue = 10, yellow };
cards.explanation: Enumerators without explicit values get previous value + 1.

cards.text: To set explicit values: enum Color { red {{c1::= 5}}, green {{c2::= 10}} };

cards.front: What are two main problems with unscoped enumerations?
cards.back:
• Name pollution (enumerators in same scope as enum)
  • Implicit conversion to int (loses type safety)

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
enum Color { red, green };
enum Fruit { apple, red };
cards.answer: Compile error: 'red' defined in both enums causes naming conflict.
cards.explanation: Unscoped enumerators pollute the enclosing scope.

cards.question: Why does this compile despite being logically wrong?
cards.answer: Both implicitly convert to int (both 0), so it compares integers.
cards.explanation: Unscoped enumerators implicitly convert to int, losing type safety.

cards.text: Unscoped enumerators {{c1::implicitly convert}} to int, causing {{c2::type safety}} issues.

cards.front: What happens when you output an unscoped enumerator with std::cout?
cards.back:
It prints the integer value, not the name.
Example: red prints as 0

cards.explanation:
Unscoped enums have issues with namespace pollution and implicit conversion. Solutions:
  • Prefer enum class (scoped enums)
  • If using unscoped, prefix names (color_red)
  • Be aware of implicit int conversions

cards.front: What naming convention helps avoid unscoped enum conflicts?
cards.back:
Prefix enumerator names with the enum type:
enum Color { color_red, color_green };
Or use enum class instead.

cards.text: By default, the first enumerator has value {{c1::0}}, and each subsequent enumerator has value {{c2::one greater}} than the previous.
Unscoped enumerators are placed in the {{c1::same scope}} as the enumeration, causing potential {{c2::naming conflicts}}.
When printing an unscoped enumerator, std::cout outputs the {{c1::integer value}}, not the {{c2::enumerator name}}.
By default, the first enumerator has value {{c1::0}}, and each subsequent enumerator has value {{c2::one greater}} than the previous.
Unscoped enumerators are placed in the {{c1::same scope}} as the enumeration, causing potential {{c2::naming conflicts}}.
